project_overview.project_name: Improving Healthcare Services in Somalia Project ('Damal Caafimaad')
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve the coverage of essential health and nutrition services in Project areas and strengthen stewardship capacity of Ministries of Health.
project_overview.total_financing: US$100 million (IDA Grant: SDR 52.3 million / US$75 million equivalent + GFF Grant: US$25 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: 1.84 million direct beneficiaries in five target regions (Nugaal, Bakool, Bay, Hiraan, Middle Shebelle); mothers, children, women of reproductive age, IDPs, nomads, and Ministry of Health officials at FGS and FMS levels

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes – Somalia is classified as a fragile and conflict-affected state. Middle Shebelle has higher documented insecurity and was added at FGS request with implementation guided by security risk assessment. Lower Shabelle and Lower Juba were excluded due to inaccessibility (accessibility rate ≤0.30). Active armed insurgency persists nationwide. Over 2.6 million internally displaced persons. Ongoing al-Shabaab insurgency and political instability, including contested elections as of May 2021.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Expanding the coverage of high-impact health and nutrition services in select geographic areas
key_components.description: Finances delivery of essential health and nutrition services through Government contracting of NGOs in public facilities across five target regions. Services include child health (immunization, micronutrient supplementation), maternal/neonatal health (ANC, BEmONC, CEmONC, family planning), GBV services, disease surveillance, communicable/non-communicable disease prevention, first aid, and mental health. Includes Third Party Monitoring (US$5 million) for nationwide EPHS monitoring.
key_components.financing: US$75.5 million (IDA: US$57.0 million; GFF: US$18.5 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Strengthening Government's stewardship to enhance service delivery
key_components.description: Supports development of stewardship capacities at Federal and FMS levels across four sub-components: (2.1) HMIS and Data Use for Decision Making; (2.2) PFM, Contract Management, and Health Financing; (2.3) Private Sector Development and Regulatory Reforms; (2.4) Organizational Development.
key_components.financing: US$11.5 million (IDA: US$6.5 million; GFF: US$5.0 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project management, knowledge management and learning
key_components.description: Supports day-to-day project management, coordination, administration, procurement, M&E, environmental and social risk management, and learning/knowledge-sharing across FGS, FMS, and Somaliland.

Allows rapid reallocation of project proceeds in the event of a natural or man-made disaster or health outbreak. Triggered by government declaration of emergency. Disbursements against approved list of critical goods, services, and works.
key_components.financing: US$0 (zero allocation at appraisal; funded through reallocation)

key_components.description: Reserved financing for Somaliland contingent on: agreement between FMoH and Somaliland on project activities; agreement on fund flows between FGS and Somaliland Ministries of Finance; and completion of fiduciary/ESF requirements. If conditions not met by MTR, IDA reserves right to cancel.
key_components.financing: US$7.0 million (IDA: US$5.5 million; GFF: US$1.5 million)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Health (FMoH) as implementing agency; Federal Ministry of Finance (FMoF) as grant recipient. Project Coordination and Implementation Unit (PCIU) established within FMoH as condition of effectiveness. Project Management Teams (PMTs) at each FMS Ministry of Health. External Assistance Fiduciary Section (EAFS) within FMoF Office of Accountant General handles FM.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (quarterly, chaired by FMoH Director General, attended by FMS Directors General and FMoF Director General); Project Leadership Committee (bi-annual, chaired by Federal Minister of Health with FMS Ministers); Project Monitoring Meetings at FMS level (quarterly); GFF Country Platform for partner coordination; Subsidiary agreements between FGS and each FMS. PCIU acts as secretariat for governance structures.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (November 2020 edition). Special procurement arrangements under paragraph 12 of Section III of World Bank Policy on IPF (FCV country status). All procurement treated as prior review by World Bank. STEP system used for all procurement transactions. PPSD and 18-month Procurement Plan prepared. Main activities: NGO service delivery contracts, TPM firm, PCIU/PMT staff, goods. Procurement risk rated High.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Three-pronged approach: (1) Regular World Bank implementation support missions (at least biannual); (2) MoH routine monitoring via DHIS2 system; (3) Third Party Monitoring (TPM) firm contracted by Government for nationwide EPHS monitoring including annual household surveys, annual health facility assessments (balanced scorecard), and ongoing electronic data collection. TPM is primary data source for Results Framework. Semi-annual monitoring bulletin required as citizen engagement indicator.

identified_risks.risk_description: Unique socio-political dynamic with contested political situation. Elections scheduled for February 2021 not held; heightened insecurity in Mogadishu as of May 2021. Risk of political stagnation, competition over scarce resources, renewed violence causing delays. Governance issues in FMoH uncovered April 2020 led to leadership changes. Somalia ranked last (180/180) in Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index 2019.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular and systematic efforts to encourage collective project actions at technical levels between FGS and FMS. Regular communication on project activities, allocations, and processes. Anti-corruption legislation passed September 2019. EAFS oversight. Strong PFM support.

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited government capacity to respond to external shocks. Economy dependent on agriculture vulnerable to climate shocks. COVID-19 further constraining fragile health system. Economy contracted 0.4% in 2020; 2021 growth projected at 2%. Low tax-to-GDP ratio of 2.5%. Government expenditure-to-GDP ratio of 12.4%.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ongoing work with IMF and development partners to support increased revenue mobilization. Project maintains fund flows for essential health services using Government fiscal mechanism.

identified_risks.risk_description: Health Sector Strategic Plan (2017-2021) implementation faces challenges due to lack of prioritization, limited implementation capacity, and inadequate funds. RMNCAH Strategy validated but implementation constrained.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Substantial capacity building and institutional strengthening interventions. Government health budget increase through RCRF. GFF coordination platform for partner alignment.

identified_risks.risk_description: First World Bank-financed health project for FMoH; limited experience in preparation and implementation. Nascent stewardship structures and health sector capacity gaps compound risk of significant implementation delays.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Findings from ongoing ASA and GFF IC preparations incorporated in design. Recruitment of best technical consultants at PCIU, FGS, and FMS levels. Phased implementation approach.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Extremely weak institutional and management capacity in health sector. Severely limited government resources due to nascent institutions and off-treasury partner support. Inadequate M&E arrangements especially in rural and security-compromised areas. Governance challenges in FMoH highlighted in April 2020.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank mobilized resources to fill institutional capacity gaps prior to project commencement. Substantial project funds allocated to strengthening government institutions and building capacity. PCIU established as condition of effectiveness. Phased implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: FM capacity challenges in FMoH likely to affect project. Potential financial mismanagement, fraud, lack of transparency, and corruption. Recent episode of fiduciary misuse in FMoH. Low levels of trust in government institutions. Decentralized payments in remote areas create accountability risks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of EAFS units under Accountant General's Department. Contract management function within PCIU with qualified specialists. Annual external audits of contractors and project. TPM for verification. PFM capacity building under Component 2. All procurement treated as prior review.

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental risks from health facility rehabilitation, medical waste generation (infectious, pharmaceutical, mercury-containing items), greenhouse gas emissions. Social risks include exclusion of vulnerable groups, social tension over service siting, conflict/security risks for health workers and project staff, labor risks including SEA/SH, GBV risks, and challenges with inclusive community consultation. SEA/H risk assessed as Substantial.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework, Medical Waste Management Plan, Stakeholder Engagement Plan, GBV Action Plan, Labour Management Procedures, Security Management Plans. Full-time Environmental and Social Safeguard consultants at PCIU federal level; Social/GBV consultants at PMT in three FMS. Security assessment of all project areas by reputable security firm. Contractor selection requires demonstration of E&S safeguard capacity.

identified_risks.risk_description: Lack of trust between key groups in society, high contestation and violence. Fragmented donor support intensifies political divisions. Selection of project target areas and fund allocation may be contentious and cause backlash from some FMS.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong involvement of FGS and FMS authorities throughout project preparation. GFF-supported country platform for improved stakeholder coordination. Close engagement with Office of Prime Minister in addition to MoH. Project GRM to manage community grievances.

identified_risks.risk_description: Active armed insurgency (al-Shabaab), ongoing conflict in multiple regions. Middle Shebelle has higher documented insecurity. Heightened insecurity in Mogadishu as of May 2021. Security risks include targeting of health centers/workers by disgruntled individuals or conflict parties. World Bank staff cannot be deployed in-country due to volatile security situation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Security risk assessment to guide implementation plan for each region especially Middle Shebelle. Security Management Plans prepared by PCIU; site-specific security plans by contractors. PCIU security advisor works with contractors. Project target area selection used Polio accessibility data to prioritize more accessible areas. Regions with accessibility rate ≤0.30 excluded.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes – Project-level GRM established, sensitized to receive and respond to sexual abuse, harassment, exploitation, and disproportionate use of force among females. Community and patient feedback collected through annual surveys and digital cell phone-based platforms. Semi-annual monitoring bulletin summarizing TPM results including beneficiary feedback and actions taken to address grievances (citizen engagement indicator in Results Framework). World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel also available. GRM complicated by difficulties accessing rural areas and collective nature of traditional complaints handling.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project is gender-tagged. Addresses high gender disparities including 99.2% FGM prevalence, high GBV rates (36% intimate partner violence, 17% non-partner violence), limited women's access to education (75% with no education), and barriers to healthcare (42% need permission to access services). Key measures: GBV services package (awareness raising, case identification, counselling, management); requirement for female staff at all facilities; family planning/birth spacing services; tracking of female OPD visits; GBV services delivery tracked as IRI; GBV Action Plan in ESMF; GBV/Social consultant at PMT level in three FMS.
vulnerable_groups: Women (particularly those facing GBV, FGM, barriers to healthcare access)
Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) – over 2.6 million displaced

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Target area selection used Polio accessibility data to prioritize more accessible/less conflict-affected areas; regions with accessibility rate ≤0.30 excluded. Middle Shebelle included at FGS request with implementation guided by security risk assessment. Security Management Plans required at regional and site levels. NGO contracting modality chosen specifically because government lacks capacity to deliver services directly in conflict-affected areas. CERC included for rapid response to emergencies. Implementation support relies on virtual means due to inability to deploy World Bank staff in-country. Lessons drawn from Afghanistan and South Sudan health service delivery in FCV contexts. Project aligned with World Bank FCV Strategy 2020-2025.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Somalia is among the world's poorest countries (GDP per capita US$327). 30+ years of conflict. Nascent federal government established 2012. Active armed insurgency. Unresolved constitutional issues between FGS and FMS. Somaliland's politically complex relationship with FGS. Weak service delivery capacity. Fiscal capacity constraints (tax-to-GDP 2.5%). Recurrent natural disasters (droughts 2016-17, 2019; floods 2019-20; desert locust invasion 2020). 2.6 million IDPs. COVID-19 pandemic straining already fragile health system. FMoH governance challenges (fiduciary misuse April 2020). Somalia ranked last in Transparency International CPI 2019. Health indicators among world's worst (MMR 692/100,000; under-5 mortality 128.4/1,000). Per capita health expenditure US$6/year vs Sub-Saharan Africa average US$81.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: World Bank staff cannot be deployed in Somalia due to volatile security situation. No World Bank staff stationed in Mogadishu. Implementation support relies on virtual means and missions to Nairobi. Security risk assessment required for all project areas, especially Middle Shebelle. Security Management Plans (SMPs) prepared by PCIU at regional level; site-specific security plans by contractors. PCIU security advisor coordinates with contractors. Heightened insecurity in Mogadishu as of May 2021 due to contested elections. High probability of security incidents during project life. TPM must access full country including areas outside government control. Digital monitoring tools used to reduce need for physical presence. Procurement accounts for high price bids due to security conditions.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes – Over 2.6 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) due to prolonged conflict and recurrent natural disasters (droughts 2016-17, 2019; floods 2019-20). IDPs are explicitly identified as target beneficiaries and included in project area selection criteria (populations with more IDPs, nomads, and rural/urban mix weighted in geographic selection index). 2012 drought caused 250,000+ deaths and displaced 2.1 million; 2017 drought displaced additional 2.1 million; 2019 drought displaced 53,000 additional people. No refugee component specifically mentioned, but IDP inclusion is central to project design.
